sources: DBE Nov 2021/2022 P2 + Jun 2021 P2 (structure check)
structure_notes: The paper is ±10–11 questions, 150 marks, 3 hours, and ALWAYS opens with statistics (Q1–Q2, ±20 marks), then analytical geometry (Q3–Q4, ±40), trigonometry (Q5–Q8, ±50), and euclidean geometry last (Q9–Q11, ±40).
From 2024: 'Provide reasons for your statements' is printed above the geometry questions — statement-reason format is compulsory and reasons carry marks.

Theorem PROOFS are examinable: 2024 asked for the full proof of the proportionality theorem (6 marks). Learn the examinable proofs: line from centre ⊥ chord, angle at centre, cyclic quad opposite angles, tan-chord, proportionality, similarity.
One analytical geometry question is triangle/quadrilateral work (gradients, lengths, ratios, areas); the other is ALWAYS the circle: equation, tangent, and a proof-flavoured final part.

Trig is four questions: (1) no-calculator exact values from a point/quadrant info, (2) identity derivation + proving, (3) general solution or interval equation, (4) graphs + reading solutions off graphs, and (5) the 2D/3D sine-cosine-area rule problem (±9-13 marks).
The last euclidean question is the hardest thing in the paper: a multi-circle rider chaining tan-chord, cyclic quads and similarity into a product-of-sides proof like GF² = BC·FC·AF/AD.
Learners lose the most marks on: reasons omitted in geometry, quadrant signs in no-calculator trig, and not restricting/checking domains in general solutions.

archetypes.name: Least-squares regression + prediction
archetypes.how_asked: Bivariate table (often with scatter plot): find the regression line ŷ=a+bx by calculator, write down r, predict y for a given x, comment on interpolation vs extrapolation.
archetypes.notes: State a and b to at least 2 decimals and keep them stored unrounded for the prediction. Know the difference between correlation strength (r) and causation.

archetypes.name: Mean/σ and the effect of data changes
archetypes.how_asked: How does adding a constant to every value (or an outlier) affect the mean / standard deviation / median? 2024: every player does 5 more push-ups — effect on σ?
archetypes.notes: Adding a constant: mean shifts, σ UNCHANGED. Multiplying: both scale. Outliers hit the mean hard, the median barely.

archetypes.name: Ogive (cumulative frequency) reading
archetypes.how_asked: Given an ogive: estimate the median and quartiles, IQR, how many observations exceed a value, and a follow-up percentage or rule-based calculation.
archetypes.notes: Median at n/2 on the cumulative axis, read DOWN to the x-axis. The steepest section is the modal class.

archetypes.name: Five-number summary / box-and-whisker
archetypes.how_asked: Compute the five-number summary from raw data, draw the box plot (answer book grid), comment on skewness, or test for outliers with 1,5×IQR.
archetypes.notes: Skew direction = the longer whisker/side of the box. Outlier fence: Q1-1,5×IQR and Q3+1,5×IQR.

archetypes.name: Triangle/quadrilateral coordinate chain
archetypes.how_asked: Given vertices (one sometimes unknown): gradient of a side, its equation, show a point value, lengths, midpoints, prove parallel/perpendicular, ratios like DB/DC, and an area-ratio part using a parallel line.
archetypes.notes: The chain builds on itself — an early error poisons later parts, so verify each value. Area ratio with a parallel line → similar triangles with ratio (side ratio)².

archetypes.name: Angle of inclination
archetypes.how_asked: Find the inclination θ of a line, or the angle between two lines via their inclinations.
archetypes.notes: tan θ = m; for negative gradients θ = 180° - reference angle. Angle in a triangle between lines = difference of inclinations (exterior angle logic).

archetypes.name: Circle: equation, centre-radius, show-that
archetypes.how_asked: Show the circle's equation is x²+y²+Dx+Ey+F=0 given centre/point, or complete the square to find centre and radius; show a point parameter like d=7 lies on the circle.
archetypes.notes: Complete the square carefully — half the coefficient, square, balance. A 'show that' means every step must appear.

archetypes.name: Tangent to a circle
archetypes.how_asked: Equation of the tangent at a given point on the circle; or prove two tangents/chords are perpendicular.
archetypes.notes: Tangent ⊥ radius at the contact point: m_tangent = -1/m_radius. For perpendicularity proofs multiply the two gradients and show -1.

archetypes.name: Level-4 coordinate hunt
archetypes.how_asked: Find unknown coordinates from a gradient AND a length (e.g. gradient of AD = -4 and AD = √612), or a circle-meets-line condition with two unknowns.
archetypes.notes: Two conditions → two equations. Distance gives a quadratic — pick the solution matching the stated quadrant/diagram.

archetypes.name: No-calculator exact values from a point/quadrant
archetypes.how_asked: A terminal point like P(-3;-4) defines angle A: find cos A, cos 2A, then sin(A-B) where B has a given ratio and quadrant.
archetypes.notes: Draw the triangle, get r, mind the SIGNS per quadrant. For compound values, write the expansion first, then substitute exact ratios.

archetypes.name: Express in terms of p/t
archetypes.how_asked: Given cos α = p (or sin 22° = p): express another value — like a compound/half-angle product — in terms of p.
archetypes.notes: Reduce the target to functions of α using co-functions, reduction and double/half-angle identities before substituting p.

archetypes.name: Reduction-formula simplification
archetypes.how_asked: Simplify a fraction of terms like cos(90°+x), sin(180°-x), cos(360°+y), sin(-y) down to a single ratio or prove it equals e.g. tan(x+y).
archetypes.notes: Convert every term to functions of x (or x and y) with correct signs, then look for compound-angle patterns in the result.

archetypes.name: Prove an identity (+ where undefined)
archetypes.how_asked: Prove an identity mixing double angles and factorising, e.g. (cos2A + cosA + 1)/(sin2A + sinA) = 1/tanA; sometimes derive cos(x+y) from a given identity, and state where the identity is undefined.
archetypes.notes: Work one side only. Choose the cos2A form that factorises with what's next to it. Undefined where any denominator (or tan) is zero/undefined.

archetypes.name: General solution / interval equation
archetypes.how_asked: Solve a trig equation (quadratic in sin/cos, co-function like sin(x+20°)=cos(2x), or with a surd wrapper) — general solution with k∈ℤ, or all x in a stated interval.
archetypes.notes: Two solution families for sin/cos equations — don't drop the second. Finish by listing solutions inside the interval if one is given.

archetypes.name: Trig graphs: draw, read, intersect
archetypes.how_asked: Given/draw graphs like tan x and cos 2x + 1 on [-180°;180°]: asymptotes, period, where f(x)≤0, and 'use the graphs to determine the general solution of' an equation combining them.
archetypes.notes: The 'use the graphs' part converts an ugly equation (e.g. 2cos³x - sinx = 0) into 'where do the drawn graphs intersect' — spot the connection.

archetypes.name: Max/min of a trig expression
archetypes.how_asked: Maximum value of an expression like (4 - 8sin²x)/3 and the smallest x where it occurs.
archetypes.notes: Rewrite with cos2x (range [-1;1]) and reason from the range — no calculus needed.

archetypes.name: 2D/3D sine-cosine-area rule problem
archetypes.how_asked: A building/mast/field scenario across 2-3 triangles with angles of elevation: calculate a horizontal distance, then a slant length or height via sine/cosine rule; sometimes prove a formula in terms of given angles first.
archetypes.notes: Solve triangle by triangle; the shared side is the bridge. Right angles hide in 'vertical building on horizontal plane'. Carry exact values between steps.

archetypes.name: Circle-theorem angle chase
archetypes.how_asked: A circle diagram (cyclic quad, tangent, diameter, centre): calculate labelled angles with reasons, e.g. exterior angle of cyclic quad = 86° chains into isosceles and same-segment angles.
archetypes.notes: Every statement needs its reason in brackets. Mark equal angles on the diagram as you go; look for tangent pairs, diameters (90°), and equal chords/arcs.

archetypes.name: Prove sides/angles equal or lines parallel
archetypes.how_asked: Hence prove AD = DC, or that a quadrilateral is cyclic, or two lines parallel — building on the angle chase before it.
archetypes.notes: Equal sides come from equal angles (isosceles) or congruency; cyclic from converse theorems. 'Hence' = use the previous answer.

archetypes.name: Bookwork: prove an examinable theorem
archetypes.how_asked: Prove the theorem: a line parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two sides proportionally — or another of the examinable proofs (angle at centre, tan-chord, cyclic quad).
archetypes.notes: Pure bookwork marks — memorise the constructions (heights/joins) and the proof skeletons for all examinable theorems.

archetypes.name: Proportionality & midpoint applications
archetypes.how_asked: With parallel lines / a centre-perpendicular bisecting a chord: prove a ratio like OR:WS = 1:2, then calculate a length given one value (ST = 15 → PT).
archetypes.notes: Perpendicular from centre bisects the chord — that plus midpoint theorem builds most 1:2 ratios. Set up the proportion, then substitute numbers.

archetypes.name: Similar triangles → product of sides
archetypes.how_asked: Prove △AGF ||| △ABC, then prove a product relation like AG·AD = AC·AF, building to a final composite like GF² = BC·FC·AF/AD.
archetypes.notes: Two angles equal ⇒ similar (AAA). Write vertices in corresponding order, form the side ratios, cross-multiply. The final part usually multiplies/divides TWO earlier results.

archetypes.name: Tan-chord and two-circle riders
archetypes.how_asked: Two circles touching with a common tangent: find several angles equal to x with reasons — tan-chord in each circle plus same-segment links.
archetypes.notes: The common tangent feeds tan-chord into BOTH circles. Label systematically; each equal angle needs its own reason.
